The aim of this course is to raise awareness of, and to explain the basic principles of Building Information Modelling (BIM). The course will enable you to understand the concepts of BIM and how the UK is building its capability and approach to deliver ‘UK BIM framework’. It also introduces the more detailed BSI courses which focus on specific aspects of ISO 19650.

This two-day course will also cover the management principles for organizations as well as key drivers for success and Return on Investment (ROI) that can be achieved. The aim is to ensure delegates complete the course with a basic understanding and overall knowledge of the processes, practice and management in utilizing Collaborative BIM on their projects. Delegates will also learn about the core suite of British and International Standards that support BIM, not only during the design and construction stages but through to the in-use stages of a project and how these have been created and developed. The course aims to evaluate why the value of the ‘whole life’ of a project supersedes that of the delivery stages and how BIM and the concept of soft landings can help to achieve these goals.
Upon completion of this course, you will be able to:
Identify the basic principles of BIM
Define and explain key terminology relating to BIM
Describe the terms "BIM according to ISO 19650" and "UK BIM framework"
Recall the definition of BIM and explain its importance
Explain the value of information throughout all stages of a project and asset lifecycle
Interpret how to apply the standards that support the "UK BIM framework" in practice
Discuss why BIM is a key factor in delivering projects on time while minimizing waste and inefficiencies
Summarize the general concept of the "Information Delivery Cycle" as outlined in ISO 19650-1
What does the term "Building Information Modelling (BIM)" mean to you, and how do you think it could relate to your current or future role in the industry?

BIM consists of a series of individual discipline models, documentation and data and is a process by which all of the information is gathered to provide more value-added solutions for the client at handover stage. The diagram above indicates how BIM is tied together by digital information, but the processes, procedures and people must be put in place to ensure the process runs as smoothly as possible. This process involves a series of individual discipline models that come together to form a ‘federated’ model for coordination and use. BIM is defined as a process rather than an object. The process must be understood by all parties involved on a project. It is extremely important that the BIM strategy of the project is known by all those involved in order for each party to achieve the relevant return on investing in this process. Further in the course we will explain how the processes, procedures and people are critical to the success of a BIM project, but in order to understand why it is relevant to discuss what BIM entails and then dissect how it positively affects our work.
In the UK alone, there are close to three million people who work in construction and therefore to upskill this number of individuals is a significant task for our industry.

In the UK alone, there are close to three million people who work in construction and therefore to upskill this number of individuals is a significant task for our industry. To be able to implement BIM for your company it may be necessary to influence key personnel to ensure they have the relevant drive to be able to push the implementation process across others. You can then feed this knowledge to your supply chain in order to support the process of training and managing BIM.Depending on the size or nature of the company you work for, it may be necessary to ensure that these individuals act as the BIM advocates, promoting and working towards the goal for the growth and profitability of the company. Critical drivers for BIM are the ‘enablers’ and these people will ensure they share, support and provide guidance in regard to your company and projects.
A BIM model (information model) – geometry, data and documentation

An information model may contain:Physical entities documenting the building/facility, systems, product types and instancesSpatial entities documenting the site, zones, storeys/regions, spaces/locations Process entities documenting the project, and for 4D work-packages, job types and tasksAll of these can be located and related together, so it is not just a list. For example, an instance may have:Multiple files of each category come together to form the building information modelThe building information model is the ‘single source of truth’The preferred term is information modelDuring a project it is referred to as a ‘project information model’, during operation it is referred to as an ‘asset information model’
Attributes and properties such as ‘Loadbearing’ TRUE
Associated material STEEL/S235JR, classification and documentation
Relationship to their named assembly ‘Steel Assembly’ (or a Relationship to a named storey/region, space/location
Relationship to their system (group or collection)
Other shapes such as plan symbols (not shown)
Relationship to their product type ‘HEA500’ (not shown)
Measured quantities such as 0.1 m3 and 1159 kg
Relationship to design, construction or in-use tasks

Refers to 3D representations of buildings, components, and objectsEnables visualization, animation, and design coordinationHelps stakeholders visualize the design early and resolve issuesBoth files with geometry and information models can be 3D
Refers to 2D drawings generated from a 3D model or non-BIM drafting softwareUsed to convey design intent and produce construction drawingsBoth drawings and information models can be 2D

BIM depends on the use of discrete ‘objects’ corresponding to the real world and which can carry both shape and attributes. The extent of this content determines the dimension (D).BIM has been defined in simple terms by a series of BIM dimensions that generally align to deliverables. The dimensions are described as:2D relates to the 2-dimensional drawings that are generated from a 3D model in a variety of file formats. 2D drawings may also be generated from non-BIM enabled software such as drafting software to convey intentions and produce drawings. An information model can be 2D, but so can a drawing.3D relates to the 3-dimensional representation of a building and its components and objects. The 3D information can be used to convey design intent as well as visualization and animation. It enables project stakeholders to be able to visualize the building very early in the design process as well as resolve any design coordination issues with ease. An information model can be 3D, but so can a file containing just geometry.

Adds the time dimension to models (days, weeks, or schedules)Includes design, procurement, and construction timelinesInformation may come from process models like Gantt chartsHelps predict construction duration, detect clashes, and manage costs
Extend the model with purposes like cost estimation or maintenance planningTypically require additional data (materials, labour, maintenance, etc.)Less standardized compared to 2D, 3D, and 4D)

4D relates to the time aspect. In simple models, days or week numbers are assigned to instances. In fuller models, processes and their precedence are included, scheduling information when an element will be designed, procured and most usually built. Information for 4D may be acquired from process models such as Gantt charts. This supports exercises to predict project construction time, detect likely workspace clashes in the workspaces and health and safety issues, and ultimately reduce unnecessary costs. Other dimensions (5D, 6D, …. 12D) are not very meaningful but try to tie in different purposes or uses for the information model. This usually requires extra information, about materials, labour and costs to tasks, or maintenance processes.
Improving cost and productivity performance in construction
These charts relate to US data, but cost and productivity problems exist in many other countries

Historically the construction sector has performed poorly in terms of cost and productivity on projects. The industry runs the risk of lagging further behind others and falling well behind the performance of the manufacturing and the automotive sectors. As we can see from the diagrams above, new construction costs have increased at a faster rate than other industries. These charts were produced by the US National Institute of Building Sciences, in 2007.Productivity in our sector is also poor, with productivity in the 2000s lower than it was in the 1960s. There are likely to be many reasons for this, including lack of skills, no investment in training for advanced process such as design for manufacture and assembly, poor use of technologies and continued reliance on manual/human processes rather than automation.Higher costs and lower productivity are clearly linked to each other, and it is believed that adopting standardized information management processes will improve the position by taking wasted effort out of design and construction processes.
Reduce construction and whole life costs
The World Economic Forum published its Action Plan to accelerate BIM adoption in February 2018. This was prepared in collaboration with Boston Consulting Group, the UK Government, Arup and Atkins.
An Action Plan to Accelerate Building Information Modelling (BIM) Adoption

The key parts of the action plan are:MotivationApproach BIM as the essential first step to digitalizationThink of BIM as a value creator, not as a cost factorArticulate BIM’s benefits across the entire lifecycleCollaborationUse integrated contracts and redefine risk-return mechanismsSet up early collaboration and communication among stakeholdersEstablish data sharing standards and open systemsEnablementEstablish BIM skills along the full value chainChange behaviours and processes, not just technologyMake a long-term commitment and support innovative funding

The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are a set of 17 global objectives established by the United Nations in 2015 as part of the ‘2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development’. They aim to address pressing global challenges, including poverty, inequality, Climate Change, environmental degradation, and access to justice, with the overarching goal of fostering sustainable, inclusive growth for all. Each SDG is interconnected, reflecting the complex and interdependent nature of social, economic, and environmental systems. In particularly to the AECO industry are;SDG 9Industry, innovation, and infrastructure aims to build resilient infrastructure, promote inclusive industrialization, and foster innovation to drive sustainable economic growth and reduce inequalities globally. SDG 11Sustainable cities and communities focuses on making cities inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable, addressing urban challenges like housing, transportation, and disaster resilience.  SDG 12Responsible consumption and production emphasizes reducing waste, improving resource efficiency, and fostering sustainable consumption patterns to protect the environment and ensure long-term prosperity for people and the planet.Achieving these goals requires collaboration among governments, businesses, civil society, and individuals, as well as innovative policies and technologies. The SDGs are a universal call to action, aiming not only to improve the quality of life for billions but also to safeguard the planet for future generations. https://www.un.org/sustainabledevelopment/sustainable-development-goals/

Published in 2021 and applies through to 2030Requires the client to comply with the UK BIM frameworkUK Construction Playbook sets out Government relationship with supply chains, including adoption of UK BIM Framework

On sourcing and contracting public works projects and programmes

UK: An updated Mandate to apply collaborative BIM and Information Management processes has been published by the UK Government. This is part of the Transforming Infrastructure Performance Roadmap to 2030. The Mandate is very specific and requires UK public clients to comply with the standards in the UK BIM framework – this means all Parts of the ISO 19650 series, plus other UK standards. In particular, the information management mandate requires the public client to:Assess the security sensitivity of each project or assetHave the capability to fulfil the client/asset owner duties in ISO 19650Define its information requirementsHave appropriate mechanisms to procure, receive, check and store the information it procures from supply chainsRecognize the value of the information it obtains in relation to projects and assetsMaintain the information it receives to make sure its value is sustainedThe UK BIM framework is also referenced in the Construction Playbook as one of the key strategies to improve the relationship between public clients and private sector supply chains. The Construction Playbook documents a series of approaches and strategies to enable the public sector to get better value from construction projects. Other policies promoted through the Construction Playbook include: wider use of offsite manufacturing and product platforms, early engagement with supply chains, wider use of whole life costing rather than focus only on capital investment (project cost).
In relation to information of all kinds: geometrical, data, documents:

BIM adoption is necessary for the following reasons:Managing information better: As we are sharing information and communicating more efficiently, BIM allows us to manage information more intelligently in order to achieve best results on projectsAvoiding error and waste both during design and construction: Coordination is improved with a BIM process, even when we are sharing information at key stages of a project, design teams and supply chain members should always share and coordinate the information. As BIM allows for this to be undertaken, through the use of ‘federating’ our information models and sharing data at critical stages, we can capture any coordination issues early and avoid re-work on site later, which can have significant cost and time implicationsGovernment mandate: To put it simply, Government spending is increasing and to be able to bid for work and support Government projects moving into the future, it is only wise to ensure you can deliver what they are asking for. UK: All construction projects (centrally procured) are to be delivered in accordance with the UK BIM framework. One aspect of this is for the client to include their Information Requirements in each appointment/contractPrivate sector ‘request’ for BIM on projects: Private sector clients are requesting BIM, even if some are not aware of why and need guidance and supportStakeholder engagement: The general public find it easier to understand how the buildings relate to their surroundings at all scales, the client body like to understand how they function and how the facility will be used. BIM allows for greater engagement with the use of virtual reality and digital tools to be able to view and understand how a facility functions prior to constructionBetter understanding of spatial properties: As we are ‘digital prototyping’ our assets, BIM enables a better understanding of projects and allows us to navigate spaces to understand them betterReduction of capital and in turn, operational costs: Capital costs are reduced by coordinating information and generally producing better quality information quicker. Operational costs can benefit by effectively managing consistently structured information created during design and construction for use in operationTransparency: BIM allows greater transparency of all processes creating a more ‘honest’ working environmentIn short, BIM offers value added solutions throughout the industry and it is not a question of ‘if’ BIM should be implemented within your company, but rather ‘when’.
Define value from the customer’s perspective
Identify the value stream needed to take a product/service from customer request to completed delivery
Ensure product/service activity flows through the value stream without delays
Use pull scheduling so that product/service is provided only when the customer wants it
Pursue the virtuous cycle of value creation and waste elimination towards perfection

Value: This must be defined by the customer. In construction, value-adding activities can be broadly defined as those that transform materials and information into something that the customer would be prepared to pay for. Non-value activities are those that don’t. A further category includes activities that don’t add value and that cannot be avoided at the present. Checks carried out for legal compliance are an example of the latter. Value stream: The flow from raw materials to completed project. The value stream can be mapped between activities that add value to expose waste. Value streams exist both on site and across company boundaries from all raw materials being used on the project whether they arrive on site as a raw material (aggregate) or as a finished product (a lift). Flow: In manufacturing the product flows through workstations or companies, which each adds value. In construction projects it can still represent the flow in or between companies until materials and products arrive on site for assembly. Construction’s uniqueness is partly that it is then the workforce who tends to flow over the project rather than the other way round. The construction programme itself can flow in this sense.Pull: All components and information are made and supplied at the necessary time to deliver the product or service to the customer at exactly the time the customer wants it. Perhaps this has always been the goal in construction rather than in manufacturing where mass production was pushed onto the customer. In construction one of pull’s biggest enemies is excessive unexposed risk contingency.Perfection: For both manufacturing and construction this represents an ‘ideal state’ that will never be achieved in the field. Striving for it by continuously improving through collaboratively identifying and removing waste provides the desired results.

Stages of maturity of analogue and digital information management

CDE: Common Data EnvironmentThis BIM maturity model articulates the differences between working in a BIM Environment and previous ways of working.A stage definition was put in place in ISO 19650-1, to guide and enable this process while outlining a staged process for industry to adopt and utilize while working within a BIM Environment.The stage definitions have guided industry in their ability to understand the staged approach to the development of BIM and how we’ve transitioned and will continue to move into a more digitally integrated space in building construction. The identification of ‘stages’ is intended to allowed those beginning their journey and others well on their way to define their capability in terms of BIM. UK: At present the Government’s Information Management Mandate focuses on the UK BIM framework which aligns with Stage 2 in this diagram.The Stage definitions can be summarized as follows: Stage 1Managed CAD in 2 or 3D format using basic layering with a collaboration tool providing a common data environment, possibly some standard data structures and format. Commercial data managed by standalone finance and cost management packages with no integration. Stage 2Managed environment held in separate discipline ‘BIM’ tools with attached data. Commercial data managed by an enterprise resource planning (ERP) system. Integration on the basis of proprietary interfaces or bespoke middleware. The approach may utilize 4D (time) and further properties to feed cost and operational systems. It is this stage that ISO 19650 is mostly concerned with.Stage 3Integrated and interoperable data, available in near real time across a project or asset team.Please refer to References section: How much BIM?

BIM global adoption is something that has been maturing over many years. Since the release of the international standards in 2018, ISO 19650 part one - concepts and principles of information management and part two – the delivery phase of projects the adoption of information management utilizing building information modelling, has steadily increased as many countries are recognizing the benefits that BIM and more importantly, better information management can bring. This growth in BIM and information management continues to grow with the support of further parts to the ISO 19650 series.There are considerable growth opportunities in construction globally and BIM processes and mandates have been implemented or moved forward in many parts of the world. Most developed economies now have some level of BIM initiative lead by government or by industry. BIM enables us to ensure that we will see considerable growth in a sector traditionally stifled by poor information and inefficient processes.This broader international adoption of BIM processes is being supported by the publication of the ISO 19650 information management using building information modelling. Parts one and two of this standard were published in 2018, covering the concepts and principles (Part 1) and the application to projects or delivery phase of assets (Part 2). Parts 3 and 5 were published in 2020, covering the application to the operational phase of assets (Part 3) and security-minded information management (Part 5). Part 4 was published in 2022, covering the quality criteria for the information exchange process.Part 6 was published in 2025, covering the exchange of health and safety information related to the project and asset lifecycle.The remainder of this BIM Fundamentals course concentrates on Collaborative BIM.
Global adoption of BIM: North America

NA (North America)United States: Strong BIM presence in public and private sectors. Mandates by the General Services Administration (GSA) promote efficiency and lifecycle managementCanada: Increasing adoption driven by government and industry collaboration, focusing on productivity, sustainability, and innovation in infrastructure projects
Global adoption of BIM: Latin America

LATAM (Latin America)Brazil: Leading BIM adoption with government initiatives improving construction processes, reducing delays and costsMexico and Chile: Advancing BIM to enhance construction quality and efficiency, supporting infrastructure and urban planning needs

EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa)Europe: Leading BIM adoption with mandates in the UK, Germany, and Nordic countries. Focus on sustainability and efficiency, supported by ISO 19650Middle East: Rapid BIM adoption in UAE and Qatar for large-scale projects and smart cities, enhancing project management and sustainabilityAfrica: Growing use in South Africa and Kenya to improve construction quality, project management, and resource efficiency

APAC (Asia-Pacific)Asia: Strong BIM adoption in Singapore, China, and Japan. Singapore leads with regulatory frameworks, China integrates BIM into urbanization, and Japan focuses on disaster resilienceAustralia and New Zealand: Robust BIM frameworks and mandates enhance public project efficiency and sustainability, integrating BIM with Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for better management

The UK’s drive to adopt collaborative BIM processes is coordinated by a consortium under the name “UK BIM framework” supported by:NIMA (previously known as the UK BIM Alliance) – including the buildingSMART UK and Ireland chapter, as a focus for industry and institutional engagementBSI – British Standards Institution, the UK National Standards Body representing UK at CEN and ISOAnd various government departments including the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS)The website through which all UK BIM framework resources are available is at: www.ukbimframework.org.

The adoption of standards forms a framework for any process and allows industry to be guided by an agreed process. By reducing the need for separate organizations to invent their own solutions to a common problem, standards are one aspect of implementing Lean thinking as they reduce wasteful duplication. The standards that support the delivery of BIM are key to the success of BIM implementation on all projects. Standards can be defined as follows: ISO – An internationally recognized standard that provides guidance and support a wide variety of industries globally. ISO’s can be technical reports, specifications and guidance and are used to ensure consistency of approach to products, services, processes or materialsCEN – European wide body made up of National Standards Bodies (not governments). Members are expected to withdraw national standards in conflict. Sometime the EU requests CEN prepares standards to support the EU legislationUK: BS – British Standards are increasingly used worldwide to adopt best practice principles to services, products or particular industries. They are the UK national standards and are a publication and specification or practice that can be used as guidance for the production of a product or carrying out a process, or providing a servicePAS – Publicly Available Specifications are standards that provide guidance to a process, product or service which is created out of industry requirement based on a particularly urgent need for industry. PAS documents commonly evolve to become British or International StandardsThe standards that support the delivery of BIM within the UK have been developed and updated since the release of the Government Construction Strategy in 2011. The standards provide best practice guidance in the way in which we plan, manage, deliver and use our built environment.
BS 8536 Design, manufacture and construction for operability

Collaborative BIM is supported by a series of pillars that define and encapsulate the process and requirements in order to deliver. ISO 19650 has been produced and is being further developed to support international implementation of BIM processes:Collaborative principles – ISO 19650-1:2018Capital/delivery phase – ISO 19650-2:2018Operational phase – ISO 19650-3:2020Information exchange – ISO 19650-4:2022Security – ISO 19650-5:2020Health and safety – ISO 19650-6:2025UK: UK BIM framework processes are guided via the UK publication of parts of ISO 19650 and other UK specific documents as outlined below:Collaborative principles – BS EN ISO 19650-1Capital/delivery phase – BS EN ISO 19650-2 including UK National AnnexOperational phase – BS EN ISO 19650-3:2020Information exchange – BS EN ISO 19650-4:2022 including UK National Annex recommending COBieSecurity – BS EN ISO 19650-5:2020Health and safety – ISO 19650-6:2025Operational considerations for delivery phase projects – BS 8536:2022Contractual extensions for both delivery phase and operational phase contracts – Information ProtocolClassification – Uniclass 2015 (UK implementation of ISO 12006-2)

The ISO 19650 series is a set of international standards designed to manage information through Building Information Modelling or BIM. It provides a consistent framework for effectively managing digital information across the entire lifecycle of a built asset. The series is divided into 6 key parts, each addressing a specific aspect of information management.Part 1: Concepts and principles This part establishes the foundational principles for information management using BIM. It introduces the Common Data Environment (CDE), hierarchy of information requirements, principles of federation and container naming and general requirements for organizing and sharing project information.Part 2: Project delivery phaseFocused on the design and construction stages, this standard provides detailed guidance on managing information during project delivery phase. It defines workflows, processes, and responsibilities for sharing, approving, and authorizing information in a collaborative environment.Part 3: Operational phaseThis part covers information management during the operational phase of an asset, ensuring that data remains accurate and accessible for maintenance, refurbishment, and eventual decommissioning.Part 4: Information exchangePart 4 standardizes processes for exchanging information between stakeholders. It discusses the structure, formats, and acceptance criteria required to ensure consistency and reliability in data exchange.Part 5: Security-mindednessRecognizing the importance of protecting sensitive information, this part addresses security measures for managing and sharing data associated to a security sensitive asset. It provides strategies to safeguard assets against cyber threats and unauthorized access.Part 6 : Health and safety informationThis standard outlines how information management can support health and safety.Overall, ISO 19650 promotes efficient collaboration, reduces risks, and enhances decision-making through standardized information management practices. By aligning stakeholders on processes and data structures, the series enables the successful delivery and operation of built assets.

ISO 19650 Part 1 is the standard supporting collaborative production of information in both the development and in-use stages. This standard provides methodology and best practice standard for managing, production, distribution and quality of construction information (including that generated by CAD) using a disciplined process for collaboration and a specified naming policy:Information management applied during the whole lifecycle of the assetRelevant information available across operational phase and delivery phaseInformation requirements cascadeProspective appointed parties should be reviewedInformation delivery plannedInformation deliverables reviewedCDE workflow for collaborative production, management, sharing and exchange of all informationFederated information models produced(Condensed from ISO 19650-1 Clause 13).

Requirements for information management during operational phase

ISO 19650 Part 3 applies the concepts and principles from ISO 19650 Part 1 to the operational part of the asset lifecycle, when the building or infrastructure is in use. There are many parallels between Parts 2 and 3, including the 8-step information management process.The ISO 19650 terminology is also used, including the appointing party for the asset owner or operator, lead appointed party for the lead organization in each delivery team, and appointed party for the lower-level appointments.The appointment, mobilization and information production requirements apply to any information provider, irrespective of whether this is an external contract or an in-house team, and also to any size of work in the operational phase (from small maintenance tasks up to major refurbishments). If necessary, the requirements of ISO 19650 Part 2 can be applied.
Specifying the criteria for assessing information quality
Mobilization and testing of exchange processes
Making sure that the quantity and type of information being delivered does not limit its future use
UK National Annex to define UK usage of COBie
How and when to exchange information between delivery teams and client/asset owner (appointing party)

ISO 19650-4 is the specification for the information exchange process between delivery teams (from both project and asset/facilities management) and the asset owner or project client (appointing party). It sets out the criteria to be used to assess information quality:Compliance to the rules of the Common Data Environment (CDE)Conformance – adhering to the agreed data schemaContinuity – avoiding spurious changes of information (e.g. unnecessary renaming of room spaces)Communication – proper use of agreed date formats, character sets, units etcConsistency – avoiding spatial clashes, gaps in the information record, contradictions between one piece of information and another, etcCompleteness – providing all the appropriate objects and properties to cover the immediate use of the information as well as how the information will be used in future project stages and how it will be used at later times in the future (e.g. during asset/facilities management)ISO 19650-4 also sets out the different checks and tests to be carried out during mobilization of any delivery team. It addresses steps to be taken when information is received, such as to import it into existing information, to federate it with existing discrete information or to incorporate it by linking.UK: A UK National Annex retains COBie as the vehicle for information exchange of structured information at the end of a project (delivery phase).

ISO 19650 Part 5 sets out a range of requirements concerning appropriate application of security to the management of information. It is aimed primarily at appointing parties (project clients and asset owners/operators) as these are the organizations who need to set the context for security.Proportionality regarding application of Part 5 is assessed through a triage process – an assessment of whether the appointing party’s asset and any neighbouring assets are security-sensitive. If not then no further action need be taken. But if they are then further requirements are described. This includes the development of a security strategy and a security management plan. These would be expected to affect the detailed requirements made through ISO 19650 Parts 2 and 3 to projects and operational maintenance/facilities management tasks respectively.It is also crucial that the appointing party has robust procedures for dealing with the security implications of appointed parties, including any unsuccessful tenderers who might have had access to sensitive information as part of their tender process.

Common Data Environment (CDE) – processes and tools for creating trust
Throughout the asset lifecycle and during project delivery

A Common Data Environment (CDE) is a mixture of processes and software tools. The processes are called the CDE Workflow in ISO 19650-1, and the software tools are called CDE Solutions in ISO 19650-1.Together, the CDE processes and CDE solutions provide a system that creates trust in the information. This means that all the participants using and referring to information held in the CDE can have confidence in the information.Disciplines will overlap and responsibilities may evolve, yet the process to achieve success relies on the creation of a culture that promotes open sharing and communication. In short these include: The ability to create a defined set of information deliverables for a projectThe ability to share information at all key stagesThe ability to communicate openly the information requirements and any subsequent project issues through the life of a projectThe Common Data Environment (CDE) is a means for managing the development of project and asset information throughout the lifecycle to ensure that information is managed properly and shared efficiently.
Cloud or shared server basedSecured by user identity, password and further securityHolds models and documentsArchive of all versions and activities to legal expectations

These ‘core’ features are common to many document management tools and informal arrangements. All CDE solution providers offer their own unique way to manage, share and deliver information. Once the system of choice has been established it is up to the appointing party to ensure the systems are put in place to effectively manage it – this is stated in both ISO 19650 Parts 2 and 3. This may include setting up common procedural guidelines for the organization or project team or sharing support with teams. The CDE process (workflow) manages all information in an electronic repository and regardless of the type of asset or project. The process involves ‘gates’ to provide approvals and authorizations. Information is strictly controlled according to its version and permitted use(s). A CDE solution (software tool) may use a project server, an extranet, a file-based retrieval system or other, suitable toolset as defined in ISO 19650-1 Clause 12. Effective management of the CDE should include: Processes and procedures set up to ensure that all parties using the CDE understand the standards that go with itAdding project team members and collaborators to the project must be monitored at all times Ensuring the use of the CDE is communicated to all partiesThe CDE is ultimately the responsibility of the appointing party, but responsibility may be passed onto the Tier 1 organization managing the processAn information hierarchy should be agreed to support the concepts of the CDE and the document repository

Read-only except for information containers created oneselfEnforces constant information container naming policyHolds metadata for each information container:Work in progress, shared, published, archiveRevision Classification of information container contents

The ‘definitive’ features are specifically required by ISO 19650 and central to a CDE. Access to information containers is restricted to read-only mode, except for those created by the user themselves. This ensures that individuals can manage their own contributions while maintaining the integrity of shared data.A consistent naming policy is strictly enforced for all information containers. This standardization eliminates confusion, simplifies navigation, and supports the organization of project or asset data across all stages. Each information container is associated with specific metadata, providing critical details about its status and content. Containers are categorized into distinct states, including Work In Progress or WIP, shared, published, and archive. These states reflect the lifecycle of the container, helping teams track progress and manage transitions effectively.Additionally, metadata includes revision information, ensuring that all updates are properly documented. This allows users to track changes, maintain version control, and avoid discrepancies across project deliverables.Another key component of the metadata is the classification of the container’s contents. The choice of a classification system to be used must be communicated to the appropriate parties. Together, these measures ensure a structured, transparent, and secure approach to managing information containers, supporting collaborative workflows and maintaining a high standard of data governance.
Enforce approval and authorization processesHold information management process documents/resourcesIssue tracking and messaging and notificationsFederation, checking and viewing tools

The ‘options’ are some features which may be added to create a more integrated process. Where required, The CDE solution or solutions may enforce approval and authorization processes to ensure all information is reviewed and verified by appropriate parties. This safeguards information integrity and ensures compliance with project requirements and standards.It also serves as a centralized hub for storing and managing critical information process documents and resources. These may include protocols, guidelines, workflows, and templates that standardize data management and support consistency across project teams and phases.The platform may enhance collaboration through its issue tracking, messaging, and notification capabilities. Teams can log and monitor issues, ensuring they are addressed promptly and effectively. Notifications keep stakeholders informed about updates, deadlines, and changes, fostering transparency and improving communication.The CDE further supports project coordination with federation, model checking, and viewing tools. Federation tools consolidate data from various sources into a single, unified model, allowing teams to work with a complete and accurate representation of the project. Checking tools validate the accuracy and compliance of data with design and regulatory standards, while viewing tools provide stakeholders with accessible and intuitive visualization capabilities, eliminating the need for specialized software.By integrating these functionalities, the CDE becomes a comprehensive platform that enhances collaboration, maintains data integrity, and supports the successful delivery of complex projects, ensuring all teams are aligned and working efficiently toward common goals.

ISO 19650 Part 2 and Part 3 require that a CDE enables:Naming policies so as to have a constant name for any information containerThe name should reflect authorship, federation breakdown (phases, systems, zones) and the type and format of the informationStatus codes to indicate the purpose for which recipients can use itRevision codes so as to be able to track changesUK: These conventions are documented in the UK national Annex to ISO 19650 Part 2. This annex does not automatically apply to ISO 19650 Part 3.Specialist sub contractors and designers are to use the same processes and status as the professional design development and coordination procedures. Standard codes for ‘revisions’ include the following: Versions created as WORK-IN-PROGRESS should be numbered using two-digit decimals, e.g. P01.01, P01.02, P01.03, etc.This should be changed to integer P01 when approved by the originator for sharing. Thereafter the version within WIP to develop the next revision should be numbered P02.01 and so onFormal revisions should be numbered sequentially, marking the revision as either preliminary (P0n) or contractual (C0n)Unauthorized revisions (where files/models are still being developed within the team) should be numbered sequentially with a P code for ‘Preliminary’, e.g. P01, P02, P03, etc.Revisions that have been authorized by the client and taken forward to the next stage of design, or for construction, should be numbered sequentially with a C code, e.g. C01, C02, C03, etc.It is noted that the ‘C’ notation indicates that the container can be used for construction or contractual purposes (e.g. Stage Completion).The National Annex makes provision to extend naming codes and status codes with project specific codes.

Soft Landings and Government Soft Landings
A Convergence Guide for Construction Projects

Soft Landings is about having an extended and more helpful handover process from the project delivery team to the facilities/asset management team. It helps the project team get a better understanding and focus on performance outcomes. There is an opportunity to:Promote an early involvement of the operator, operations team or facility management team during a projectExtend the commitment of the design and construction team regarding operational performance and support during the early months and years of occupation or useSoft Landings was originally defined by BSRIA and the Usable Buildings Trust and was published by BSRIA in a series of guidance documents. Government Soft Landings (GSL) took the same principles and extended them specifically for government project clients.
Differences between BSRIA Soft Landings and Government Soft Landings (GSL)
Recommends a Soft Landings Representative from within the department and delivery team.
Recommends a Soft Landings Champion per government department.
Does not require BIM to be adopted for Soft Landings projects.
Aligned with the UK BIM framework, integrating BIM as part of the process.
No specific guidance on tracking capital and operational costs.
Requires objectives for capital and operational costs to be set, reviewed, and updated.
Targets are set at the concept design stage after establishing strategic principles.
Targets are set at the strategic briefing stage early in the project.

There are a few differences between the GSL framework and the BSRIA Soft Landings framework. These include: Government recommend that a Soft Landings Champion be appointed per department. The BSRIA framework recommends that the Soft Landings Representative be appointed from within a department and then as part of the project delivery teamGSL is aligned with the UK BIM framework whereas the BSRIA Soft Landings framework does not inherently require BIM to be adopted for a Soft Landings projectGSL contract guidance requires objectives to be set up for capital and operational costs and for this to be reviewed and updated as the project progressesGSL requires targets set at the strategic briefing stage where as BSRIA recommend targets set at concept design stage. This would be following the establishment of the strategic principles

A critical component of asset management is the strategy toward updating the information as the asset changes. Therefore, a strategy must be in place for updating or revising the AIM should further information be gathered or changed.Day to day operation: Understanding what information is available and how to access and update it within the AIM is a task that anyone involved in the operation of an asset should understand. Methods of doing this will vary depending upon which system is used but the principles will be the samePlanned and reactive maintenance: From a simple spreadsheet with a notification system to a full Computer-Aided Facility Management (CAFM) system linked to the Building Management System (BMS) and on-site team mobile devices, there are processes and procedures to be put in place and followed. Again, the emphasis is different but consistently structured information alongside clear procedures to keep it up to date will ensure the system is reliable and valuableMinor works: Equipment replacement, furniture relocation or moving a partition; all of these real-life situations will require an update to the AIM. Understanding what part of the AIM needs updating and how it will be done is key to creating and communicating these proceduresMajor works: A building extension or building re-purposing may require a full project design and delivery team. Communicating the needs of the existing AIM and how the new data will fit with it, is the purpose of this taskEnd of life: Questions such as ‘what can be re-used?’, ‘what can be recycled?’, ‘are there any unsafe areas of the building?’ etc., are all questions that may be asked at the end of the building’s life. Being in possession of an up-to-date AIM will make answering these questions much easier

Expressing information requirements. This module explores how information and data is requested by the client or asset owner.As we shall see, there are different types of information requirement that build on each other. Part of this is about taking the non-technical expression of need by directors and other senior managers, and by external stakeholders, and then translating these needs into contractual requirements that can be included in a specific scope of work for a design consultant, contractor, manufacturer, asset contractor, etc.

This diagram is based on Figure 2 from ISO 19650-3, in which the arrangement of different types of information requirements was further developed from Figure 2 in ISO 19650-1.The Organizational Information Requirements (OIR) support the Asset Information Requirements (AIR) and the Project Information Requirements (PIR). The OIR are strategic information requirements based on the needs of the organization, whereas the AIR and PIR are high-level information requirements focused on a project or a particular asset/portfolio (such as overall achievement of budget targets or overall targets for asset utilization).The AIR and PIR are then used to generate Exchange Information Requirements (EIR) for both operational and project delivery parts of the lifecycle. The EIR (delivery) define what will be in the Project Information Model (PIM) and the EIR (operational) define the contents of the AIM. The project information model also contributes information to the AIM, by way of information needed for the operation and maintenance of the asset that is generated during the project. In between EIR and AIM or PIM (as appropriate), is where all the information delivery planning and production takes place, by each of the appointed parties (suppliers such as maintenance teams, designers or contractors). This diagram is therefore a very client-oriented map of the process, seen only from a high level. It is important to recognize the amount of detail that will also need to be generated by each appointed party to plan its own information delivery.

OIRs depend on the overall nature of the business and encompass all information aspects. The OIRs can be defined as the information required by a particular organization in order to enable systems or any other organizational activity. OIRs are specific to each organization. Organizations can have different needs for information and therefore these should and will be different. Identifying the OIRs will require a variety of different types of input from various departments within the organization and may also include stakeholders outside the organization. Input will be needed in particular from the people involved in the strategic decision making associated with the portfolio management, asset management and operational (facilities) management systems.This will support a successful asset management plan that is aligned into the AIRs which in turn provide clearer client needs for information from the delivery of projects.
Forms the basis for each operational phase EIR
Information relating to the operation of an asset, to help address key decisions
Needed to answer an OIR, or part of one
Need to reflect the asset and the owner/operator’s strategy towards it

Asset Information Requirements (AIRs) capture the asset-level information needs of the appointing party (asset owner/operator), so that detailed and specific Exchange Information Requirements (EIRs) can be developed. Some of the topics that AIRs can be related to include:The performance of the asset (e.g. needing regular energy cost summaries)The use of the asset (e.g. needing occupancy data)The condition of the asset (e.g. future schedule of equipment maintenance and replacements)The AIRs should be able to be linked back to some of the OIRs. This helps give line of sight from the top-level strategic aspects of the organization down through its operational and detailed contractual information requirements.It is up to the asset owner/operator (appointing party) to decide which of its assets are going to be included in its information management process and therefore need AIRs – it could decide to prioritize assets that are critical to the overall business.
Forms the basis for each delivery phase EIR
Forms guidance for appointed parties to assess their execution plans
May become more detailed as project progresses to pass critical milestones

Project Information Requirements (PIRs) may be established at critical stages of a project as required and when it is developing to move to the next stage. Information will be required at key points and therefore the appointing party will need to get those questions answered efficiently in order to make its key decisions regarding a project according to its own project governance processes. These key decision points often align with the ends of project stages, but they do not have to do this. They also do not have to cover every project stage if the governance process does not require it.In the development of a project and during the early process, the PIRs can be broad, and as the project develops become more detailed. The provision of a clear set of PIRs helps the appointing party (project client) define the project Exchange Information Requirements (EIRs) for each appointment. It might be helpful for the client to issue its PIRs to the lead appointed parties as part of their tendering, to give them some high-level background to their detailed EIRs. But there is no requirement in ISO 19650 to do this.
EIRs are the information specification for an appointment made in connection with a project or asset/facility management
Operational phase EIRs originate in the owner/operator’s asset or facility management team
Delivery phase EIRs originate in the client’s project team

Exchange Information Requirements (EIRs) are the precise specifications for what information a project client or an asset owner/operator needs to receive back from their supply chains. They address the broader issues captured in either the AIRs or the PIRs depending on whether the EIR is related to asset/facility management or project delivery.Where an asset owner/operator is also the client for a project, then its needs for maintenance and operational information are defined as EIRs by its facilities management or asset management teams and then passed across to the project delivery team to be included in the appropriate project contracts with designers and contractors.EIRs focus on what information is expected to be delivered, the timing or frequency of delivery and the acceptance criteria that the client or asset owner/operator will use to judge if the information is acceptable.
High level information needed by the appointing party
Detailed information required from a lead appointed party
Detailed information required from an appointed party

Information requirements cascade down through the supply chain – e.g. from design consultants down to specialist consultants or from main contractors down through sub-contractors. The contractor supply chain typically extends to many layers or tiers and the information requirements can cascade down through this hierarchy.At the lowest level, the information requirements may be very precise and few in number (product description, serial number, installation date).

Supports both daily operations and strategic asset managementInformation requirements and exchange methods must be clearly defined from the startRequirements apply to all activities generating operational information, from maintenance tasks to capital projects

Developed during the project’s delivery stagesIt includes both 3D geometrical models and cumulative project informationThe PIM is essential for intelligent decision-making across different project stages

An information model is a coherent set of information and data, but can be spread across many containers, formats or schemas. However, the information must be not contradictory and should be complete insofar as it is responding to a single or logical group of exchange information requirements. The Project Information Model (PIM) is created during the delivery stages. The PIM is not only a series of 3d geometrical models but contains all the cumulative information relevant the project. This forms a key component of delivering intelligently across varying stages of projects. The Asset Information Model (AIM) is the information model that supports the day-to-day and strategic management of the asset (infrastructure or building). It is important that requirements for information and how this is to be exchanged into the AIM is communicated at the very start every activity that is going to generate operational information, whether this is a standalone maintenance task or the delivery of a new capital project.At the end of a project, any contents from the PIM that are relevant for ongoing maintenance and operation, such as the manufacturer maintenance schedules for new equipment, are transferred across to the AIM.A project that is at the start of the asset’s lifecycle (to create it) will therefore provide the initial contents of the AIM. For a project that is taking place part way through the operational life (such as a major refurbishment) then the relevant parts of the PIM will add to the AIM that already exists for the asset.

To manage a BIM project effectively it is critical that the information management process consists of a structured approach. The sharing and filing of information is very important as it drives a common standard amongst all project participants and guides them toward a common process. The industry is currently going through a substantial shift, and therefore will need support in working on delivering projects in line with the concepts described in ISO 19650-1 and the requirements and processes defined in ISO 19650-2. The approach to sharing information in a common way is critical as it ensures teams have the correct information shared with them at the correct time and stage of a project to avoid any issues downstream.

A key part of ISO 19650-2 is the need for the appointing party to establish the project’s information standard, and to establish the project’s information production methods and procedures.The information standard is expected to cover a range of topics, including:How different parties on the project will exchange information, including with the appointing party. This may specify certain methods or procedures or toolsHow information is to be structured and classifiedHow the appointing party has decided to specify the level of information need for each information deliverableHow information is going to be used in the operational phase – this helps with the identification of handover information that will ultimately be imported into the AIM

BS EN ISO 19650-2 UK National Annex sets out a clear system of naming information containers:

File naming should be in line with the convention defined in the UK National Annex to ISO 19650-2. version 2: 2021. Other types of information containers such as layers have their own naming convention.The example file name shown on the slide indicates that this an information container (file) for project PR1, produced by XYZ, concerning system S1, relating to 3rd floor (or location 3), the file is a drawing (D) and relates to Civil Engineering, and it is the first such container with all of those previous codes. If another file had all the same codes, it would be numbered 0002, and so on.An information container is defined in ISO 19650-1 as:“A named persistent set of information retrievable from a file, system or application storage hierarchy”.An information container could be a sub directory, information file (such as a model, document, table, etc.) or distinct subset of an information file, such as a chapter or section layer or symbol. The term information container is non widely used, but it was important to find a term that could cover the broad range of instances. Containers can be large or small.

The purpose of a federation strategy for project or asset information is to explain how the overall project or asset information model needs to be sub-divided into manageable parts. The need for this sub-division can be for a number of reasons:Enabling a multi-party team to work collaboratively in parallel as efficiently as possibleRespecting different levels of security control for different parts of the information modelPractical limits on the speed of upload/download (transmission) to exchange information containersTo solve these problems, the federation strategy should define the manageable parts that address each relevant purpose. For example:Define spatial volumes for each design team to work within, to make sure their objects (components and equipment) do not spatially clash with other teamsDefine security classifications for each space or system in the project or asset, so that each information container can be tagged appropriately within the CDEFor transmission limits, define the maximum size of any file to be uploaded to the CDEThe sub-divisions in each federation strategy must be non-overlapping. However, there can be more than one federation strategy, and each might sub-divide the information model differently.
Geospatial reference, coordinate system and origin

ISO 19650 part 2 requires that the project information strategy should ensure that that a project should be located in its correct geographical coordinate system. The details to how this is achieved rely upon the responsibility of the supplier, though the definition of the geospatial system is the responsibility of the appointing party.The project origin should be common amongst all parties as to ensure the geographic location (regardless of the software used or managed within) ensures that information of a graphical nature can be shared and align with one another at all times. The projects should be modelled to the agreed and origin rotation. This should be related to both the project grid, the site origin and the site context and a specific geospatial north position. The project origin however should be defined within early stages of the project and located as close to the project site extent as possible. As the project will be aligned to a geospatial north and a project north this should be made aware to all parties and communicated to any suppliers entering the project. Project space should remain consistent at all times within a project’s lifecycle. Larger (infrastructure) projects (>1km) will need to consider the impact of the divergence of cartesian and geospatial locations.
‘The act or process of dividing things into groups according to their type’

Classification as a means of data management and hierarchy is a critical component of information management as it allows an individual or entity to divide elements via their type. The reason why this is important, especially in the context of BIM is that we require access to information quickly and due to the fact this is almost impossible in a non-BIM context, the need to classify will enable information to be found, shared and used as we need it. It is useful to classify ‘spaces’ in the first instance by type, and then to classify each space further by which ‘activities’ can be carried out within them. It is possible to classify the ‘systems’ which support the spaces and then the ‘products’ which form the systems. UK: Uniclass2015 is the current classification system for the UK industry covering all construction sectors. Uniclass2015 provides:A unified classification system for the construction industryA hierarchical suite of tables that support classification from a university campus or road network to a floor tile or kerb unitA numbering system that is flexible enough to accommodate future classification requirementsA system compliant with ISO 12006-2 that is mapped to NRM1 and supports mapping to other classification systems in the futureA classification system that will be maintained and updated by NBS (National Building Specification), typically every three months Note in the UK, Systems are sometimes referred to as ‘elements’Currently updated Classification tables published can be found at the following link:https://www.thenbs.com/our-tools/uniclass-2015

How to respond to information requirements

To manage a BIM project effectively it is critical that the information management process consists of a structured approach. The sharing and filing of information is very important as it drives a common standard amongst all project participants and guides them toward a common process. The industry is currently going through a substantial shift and therefore will need support in working on delivering projects in line with the concepts described in ISO 19650-1 and the requirements and processes defined in ISO 19650-2. The approach to sharing information in a common way is critical as it ensures teams have the correct information shared with them at the correct time and stage of a project to avoid any issues downstream.
Larger amounts of detailInformation delivery plansDetailed responsibility matrix
Checking that the mobilization plan is being followed and information is being delivered correctly

A supplier response to information requirements goes through a sequence of stages. The is irrespective of whether the requirements are received directly from an appointing party (in the case of a lead appointed party), or from a lead appointed party (for all other appointed parties).The initial response is when the (lead) appointed party is still in competition to win the work. This is during the appointment process. Because of this, there is a practical (and a commercial) limit to how much work can be done to plan the future information delivery. This is the point at which the capability and capacity need to be explained, as this is important for the appointing party to take into account when it makes the appointment decision.The next response is once the appointment has been made. Now the appointed party is no longer in competition with others and can justify spending more time and resource to plan its future work, as well as confirm its previous statements.Finally, during the production of the information itself, the appointed party needs to keep checking that the plan is being followed and information is being delivered according to the standards, methods and procedures that have been agreed. There is also the need to keep the plan up to date with any changes in the work being done and any changes in the team doing the work.
Pre-appointment BIM execution plan, risk assessment

Each lead appointed party will respond to the documentation received from the client or asset owner/operator – the appointing party – and put together their response.Broadly speaking, this covers:The response to the information requirements, through a pre-appointment BIM Execution Plan (BEP) that sets out their overall approach and some of their initial thinking on how to allocate responsibility for information production within their team. The response also includes an assessment of any risks associated with meeting the information requirementsEvidence and supporting information to indicate that the supplier has the ability and the resources to deliver their work using BIM and ISO 19650A plan for mobilizing their workforce and their technical resources so that they are ready to work collaboratively and efficientlyThis process should ensure that the appointing party (client or asset owner/operator) appoints a team that has the capability to be able to deliver on what it is they hope to achieve regarding BIM. Should the appointing party not have the required BIM knowledge to be able to assess whether the supply chain has the capability to deliver, it is recommended that they appoint a representative who can work alongside them to do this on their behalf.
Capability: “The ability to perform a task, including skills, knowledge, and expertise”
Capacity: “The resources available to complete a task, including personnel and organizational structures”

ISO 19650-1 Clause 8 sets out the principle of delivery team capability and capacity. This applies to delivery teams that are engaged on projects and also on facility/asset management activities.It is worth reminding ourselves what capability and capacity mean. Capability is about the ability to perform a task; having the necessary skill, knowledge, or expertise. Training and experience both contribute to developing capability at a personal level.Capacity is about the resources needed to perform a task; having enough individual members of staff to carry out the volume or intensity of work envisaged. This is about balancing available staff against the current and projected workloads from clients. There can also be organizational capacity, where the organization has the necessary processes, support structures, and commitment in place to allow the individuals to perform properly.

The high-level responsibility matrix is considered as part of the tender response for a project-based appointment. Clearly this cannot cover all information deliverables envisaged for the project, as that would be too detailed for a pre-appointment plan. However, it would include the responsibility for the elements of the information model (which could be the principal systems needing design) and the most significant/critical/challenging deliverables from each element.The detailed responsibility matrix is the next level of development from the high-level matrix. This is produced as part of the appointment activities and would indicate the content, timing and team responsibility for information production and exchange.There are no templates for these responsibility matrices in ISO 19650, but the table shown in the slide is an indication of what a detailed responsibility matrix might look like.

The responsibilities for any organization working on a project or in asset management need to be made clear in the appointment documentation. It is sensible if responsibilities related to information management are aligned and combined with other (technical) appointments, such as for design work. The responsibility matrix helps with this, but there is likely to need to be some checking that the appointment contract (or equivalent document) reflects the arrangement that the appointing party (client/asset owner) wants to put in place. It is also important to confirm arrangements with in-house staff and teams through method statements or similar.BIM deliverables and BIM activities can be included in the appointment through a special BIM protocol – the UK BIM framework has an Information Protocol which is aligned to ISO 19650.
Confirm the individuals providing information management and authoring

The composition of the delivery team has to be confirmed after the appointment is made. This gives confidence to the appointing party that the same personnel who were put forward during the tender stage are still intended to deliver the work. This is really important for the appointing party (client) who might have made the appointment partly on the basis of the personal skills and experience of the team being proposed.However, there are often going to be practical reasons why the exact same team that was originally proposed cannot be mobilized on the project. It may be that an individual has left the company, or it may be that it took the appointing party longer than expected to confirm the appointment and that in the meantime some personnel have been allocated to different projects. Either way, it is up to the appointed party, as the contracted organization, to identify replacement team members with at least similar levels of skill and experience.

In the BIM execution plan, in addition to the confirmation of organizational roles and responsibilities, and names/details of the individuals carrying out information management and authoring, there are some other details to be confirmed after the appointment has been made:The information standard, based on any negotiations with the appointing partyThe information production methods and procedures, based on any negotiations with the appointing partyThe IT solutions to be used for authoring and managing the informationIn addition to the BIM execution plan, the lead appointed party needs to confirm:Any additional EIRs that the lead appointed party wishes to include in the appointed party appointments: This is most likely to be the case where the lead appointed party is the construction lead, and the appointed parties are the trade or package sub-contractors. These extra EIRs will also be used where the lead appointed party introduces any additional information exchange into the sequence set out by the appointing party Again, this is most relevant in the main contractor/sub-contractors situationPreparation of the task information delivery plans for each task teamCompilation of a master information delivery plans from the task plans
The time the task team will need to produce information
The task team’s responsibilities within the detailed responsibility matrix
The availability of shared resources within the delivery team
Any extra information requirements from the lead appointed party
A schedule of what information is going to be delivered, by whom, how much detail is going to be provided and any successor/predecessor issues.

The format of Task Information Delivery Plans (TIDP), may be dictated by the EIR, and/or proposed in the pre-contract BEP.Task Information Delivery Plans (TIDP) are prepared by each task team, which broadly equates to each design discipline, or key contractor package (also referred to as an appointed party). As the TIDP refers to everything produced by one team, this document as a whole will define who is delivering each of the deliverables it lists. It is helpful if each TIDP is generated to the same format; spreadsheet layout or Gantt project plan so that TIDPs from different teams can be combined into a single plan as easily as possible.Each task team shall establish a TIDP and maintain this plan throughout its appointment. In doing this, each task team has to consider the items shown on screen. This is discussed in more detail in the ISO 19650-2 course.
Delivery team Master Information Delivery Plans (MIDP)
