The Master Information Delivery Plans (MIDP) enable each lead appointed party to collate the task plans across their whole delivery team. This means the timing of delivery between different task teams can be coordinated, and also that the time required for reviews and approvals can be built into the overall delivery schedule.Each MIDP is a live document within the delivery team, as whenever the delivery schedule for one task team changes, or when a new task team joins the delivery team, then those changes need to be reflected in the MIDP.
Producing open information and exchanging information

This session focuses on the production of open information, which is not tied to specific software tools or vendors, and the exchange of information within the project or asset teams. We have already introduced the collaborative working methods of the Common Data Environment (CDE) earlier in the course, but now we are looking at open data formats, checking processes for information deliverables, and the methods available to specify the amount of detail to include in an information deliverable.
Exchange of structured data between architectural/structural/building services and downstream applications

IFC stands for Industry Foundation Classes. IFC is a standard for building information models, not for drawings. It is an enabler for exchange of information about building/construction elements, other objects and information inside a geometrical information model. IFC is a neutral schema acting as an open file format with data and geometry embedded. Developed by buildingSMART (formerly the International Alliance for Interoperability) its aim is to facilitate interoperability in the construction industry across all areas of design, construction and operation. The IFC model specification is open and available to all. It is registered by ISO and is an official standard (ISO 16739:2018). IFC files are based on a view definition that determines the scope of the IFC Exchange. A Model View Definition (MVD) defines a subset of the IFC schema that is needed to satisfy one or more exchange requirements. Because of its focus on ease of interoperability between software platforms, the Danish government has made the use of IFC format(s) compulsory for publicly funded building projects. Also, the Finnish state-owned facility management company Senate Properties demands use of IFC compatible software and BIM in all their projects. The Norwegian Government, Health and Defence client organizations require use of IFC BIM in all projects as well as many municipalities, private clients, contractors and designers have integrated IFC use into their businesses.
Overview of Industry Foundation Classes architecture

The Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) data schema is made up of four layers. The domain layer allows the schema to address different project domains, including structural analysis, architecture, engineering services and different civil engineering domains. In these domains, the concepts specific to each topic are defined. For example, boilers and chillers in the HVAC domain.The interoperability layer covers different types of shared elements that are used across domains. For example, beams, columns and walls are defined in the Shared Building Elements, and asset, occupant and furniture are defined in the Shared Facilities Elements.The core layer defines abstract concepts such as space, site, building, building element, process, product.The resources layer has 21 different groups of resources in it and has been omitted from the diagram in the slide for clarity. These resources define basic properties such as geometry, geometrical relationships, materials, measurements and time.*building** management

Structured facility information for the commissioning, operation and maintenance of a project usually in a neutral spreadsheet format Used to deliver information to the employer or operator to populate decision-making tools, facilities management and asset management system

COBie is a method/schema for structuring information about a project or asset specifically around handover. Whereas IFC is used to structure information and geometry at all stages of the asset life cycle.COBie is not an international standard: It was initially developed in the USA where it is defined by the US National Institute of Building Science (NIBS) in the National BIM Specification (NBIMS). It was further developed in the UK to enable it to be applied to infrastructure projects, as part of the UK BIM framework. COBie is also supported by buildingSmart where an IFC Model View Definition has been created to implement the COBie data structure.COBie is delivered in an open file format (e.g. xlsx) and intended to be able to work with FM solutions either directly or through import routines or plug-ins. In spreadsheet view, COBie appears as a workbook with a set of interlinked worksheets or tabs. These worksheets enable the asset to be divided up into logical portions to deliver information relating to different aspects of the asset.

COBie provides a common structure for the exchange of information about new and existing facilities (both new build and infrastructure projects). A COBie file contains only one facility. In the diagram, the topics in bold relate specifically to the design aspects of the facility, populating the asset register, holding information about the visitable spatial locations (floors (regions), spaces (locations) and zones) and the manageable equipment and components, systems and types that make up the facility. The topics in red relate to the construction aspects of the facility, completing the data for actual installed components/types and documenting the maintenance jobs required, the resources required to complete each job and the spare parts handed over.

Facility: Information about the overall facility, site and project, units and descriptionFloor (regions) and so is spatial not physical: Details of the floor levels and both horizontal and vertical components of the assetSpace (locations): Defined as visitable rooms, and the associated level and data (e.g. room number)Zone: Details of the activities within spacesComponent: Individually manageable items, that have a specific location and information relating to their propertiesType: A specific type or specification (e.g. a door type) of a componentSystem: A group of equipment components that supports a functional requirementSecurity should be provided by the CDE so that sensitive areas of a project do not need to reside in a separate repository.

Information (blue) typically acquired during the ‘build’ phase, that supports operation and maintenance of items listed in the Types sheet:Spare: Associated to a Type Resource: Required by JobsJob: Any activities that may need to be undertaken during operation

The other supportive, COBie worksheets include: Instruction: Details of the title, version, release and status of the document as well as language/regionPicklists: A reference sheet for picklists being used elsewhere in the COBie fileContact: Information on the project participants and their details (name, email, organization)Assembly: A group of systemsConnection: A connection within an assemblyImpact: Metadata that defines if a component or system would need to be replaced or an activity undertaken on the elementDocument: Documentation about all elements within the facilityAttribute: Descriptions of attributes for types of componentCoordinate: Definition of coordinate systems being used in the COBie fileIssue: Risks and known uncertainties about the facility and/or the data

Compliance to the rules of the common data environment:Information container ID properly appliedInformation container metadata properly appliedSecurity controls (including visibility to other team members) properly applied

ISO 19650-4 specifies 6 criteria for checking the quality of an information model. These apply across all the different types of information that can be included in an information model: geometrical information, structured data, documentation.
Conformance:Does the information in the container conform to the specified data structure/format

Conformance demands that the information container ‘conforms’ to both the schema and format. Schema and format should have been specified, for example in the information standard or recorded in the BIM execution plan.The schema specifies the internal structure, and the format indicates what tools can be used. For example STEP physical format files can be read by all IFC viewers. Some viewers can read ifcXML format as well, but so can any XML tool.Structured information can be tested using a variety of tools.Unstructured documentation and proprietary files can only be tested by using the appropriate applications.

ContinuityCheck that information aligns with location instructions (e.g. proper use of the client/asset owner origin point for geometrical information)Check that information has not been changed from previous versions without good reason (e.g. one design practice renaming floors or rooms in a building)

Continuity means there shouldn't be any (unjustified) change to the common basis for the information model. Of course, these things can change, if the change reflects real need. But spurious change disrupts the common basis.This includes ‘physical’ and ‘temporal’ aspects such as grids and origins, but also the ‘semantic’ aspects such as the naming of the entities.Some of these may be the subject of external standards: Location may be the subject of mapping and sector conventionsDates and week numbers are standardised through ISOThere are some ISO standards affecting naming, for example building storeysProperties are standardised in the buildingSMART Data Dictionary (bsDD) and elsewhereSome sectors (such as steel) have standardised names of materials and profiles
Communication:Dates and calendars presented properlyAgreed units of measure used consistentlyAgreed character sets/encoding applied properly

Communication covers some common pit-falls relating to the communication of electronic information. Any of these issues can lead to confusion. Confusion can arise if these technical issues are not managed. For example names, with strange characters can disrupt information exchanges. This needs to be checked during mobilization and watched for during each information exchange.

Consistency:Check for overlaps or gaps within the information contents or between one information container and another (e.g. clashes between design disciplines, technical incompatibilities between adjacent materials or components)Check the coherence of attributes or properties of objects in the information containerCheck the accuracy of quantities or qualities captured in the information container

Many practitioners focus first on ‘clash detection/avoidance’ but consistency is far more wide-ranging. Geometric consistency includes eliminating ‘clashes’. Duplication is an extreme kind of ‘clash’. But there is also a need to verify that there are no gaps, particularly in the envelope, in the structure and the distribution systems. It is also possible to discuss the same issues for attribute consistency. Whilst duplication of the same value on an object may be tolerable, differences are unacceptable. Omissions of attributes may also be unacceptable. Numeric accuracy (number of significant figures) and text fields matching expected patterns should be applied consistently.

Completeness:  Check that the information container contains the quality and quantity of information expected (as described by the level of information need) Check this in relation to the immediate purpose of the information deliverable Check this in relation to the future use(s) of the information deliverable (e.g. during operation and maintenance of the asset) Check this in relation to long-term management and societal requirements (e.g. applying standard classification codes properly)

Completeness should be defined in the information standards applicable to the project delivery stage or operation trigger event. In particular the relevant EIR should be referenced. Consideration should include the short- medium- and long-term  purposes.This can include: Entities: What objects should be present, including groupings such as zones and systems and packagesRelationships: Any specific relationships (such as the link between spaces and bounding walls, slabs, windows, doors) or between linear elements (such as distribution, and paths, routes, tracks and lanes)Properties: What attributes are required on which types of entityShape: 3D and/or 2D: Level of geometryQuantities: Which can be derived from 3D, which are documented manually?Descriptions: How much explanation is needed? External references. What Documentation? Classification? Library links?
Verification and validation of information deliverables

VerificationStage 1: Verification checkEnsures the deliverable contains the expected type of informationLike checking that a labelled box contains the correct contentsExample: Verifying that a 3D model actually includes geometrical elementsVerification can be partially automated when data follows a documented standard

ValidationStage 2: Validation checkGoes beyond verifying existence and checks content accuracyExample: For a geotechnical survey requirement, validation checks the document’s content and level of detailValidation typically requires human involvement/manual review to confirm complianceThis process is harder to automate compared to verification

Apart from checking by its originator, information will also be checked by its recipient. Depending on the hierarchical structure of the team, this could be directly by the appointing party (client/asset owner) or it could also be by the lead appointed party (such as a lead designer or main contractor or lead asset contractor).It is usual for checks to be carried out in two stages. The first is a verification check to test that the information deliverable contains the expected type of information. This is like looking into a set of boxes to make sure that each box actually contains something, and that the contents match the label on the outside. In data terms this might be checking that a deliverable that is called a 3D model does contain geometrical elements. These types of checks can be automated to an extent, particularly if the format of the deliverable is to a documented data standard.The second check is validation to see whether the contents of the deliverable actually address the information requirement that was specified. This is a more technical check. For example, the information requirement could have been for a geotechnical survey report. The verification would have checked that the deliverable existed and was a document (and not a photograph or a site plan). The validation check is now examining the document and checking that it actually is a geotechnical survey to the right level of detail as per the information requirement. This is much more difficult to do automatically and so requires human reviewers to assess the deliverables and confirm that they comply with the requirements.

Level of information needISO 19650-1 Clause 11.2

Level of information need is the generic term used in ISO 19650 to describe any mechanism by which the amount of detail in an information deliverable is being defined. This is critical to the success of a BIM project or asset activity as all suppliers will be aware of what information is required of them during their appointments.
Explaining how much information is needed
ISO 7817-1First edition 2024-06Building information modelling - Level of information need Part 1: Concepts and principle
Largely geometry drivenAlso includes attribute data
ISO concepts for Level of information need
Levels of detail (geometry) defined by NBS

The issue of how much information is needed and has to be produced is central to efficient information management, since both over-supply is waste and under-supply increases risks of errors.A number of ways to explain this key point have appeared:The AIA (American Institute of Architects) Level of Development specification defines a numerical scale (LOD100, LOD 200, LOD300, LOD350, LOD400, LOD500) which are tied into the principal stages in the project development and delivery. LOD started as a mainly geometrical definition of deliverables (how much detail should be in a BIM object at the various levels) but it has now expanded to include data descriptions for these levels as wellISO has published its standard ISO 7817 Part 1 which sets out concepts and principles for defining Level of information need. This identifies characteristics of geometrical, alphanumerical and documentation information. Further parts of the standard are being prepared to provide more detailIn the UK, NBS developed a wide range of geometrical descriptions related to different products and systems and illustrated these for different project stages. This is called Level of Detail (LoD). NBS (National Building Specification) is a UK-based business providing construction specification information used by architects, engineers and other building professionals to describe the materials, standards and workmanship of a construction project
…a term coined to suggest the negative impact of too much information. It is particularly prevalent in 3D geometrical models where attribute data is attached to the objects.

The geometrical model should only contain enough information/data to support the stage that the project is at, or the amount of information that the appointing party (client) has specified through their EIRs. Sharing model data via the CDE will require thorough quality checking and auditing processes that are in place for the particular supplier to ensure that there is no superfluous data associated to elements that should not be there. BIM is only successful if all suppliers are aware of their responsibilities and ensure the information is gathered, managed and shared correctly, even during Work in Progress stages of a project. A typical example of ‘too much information’ is outlined below: Data embedded in model element such as a door with a specific manufacturers product number or code embedded in the metadata at concept design. Model elements are not to be relied upon for manufacturers detail at early stages, and in particular in concept stage design.If too much information is appearing in geometrical models, it might be because designers/authors are picking up objects from manufacturer’s catalogues and using them as placeholders before the actual component selection has been made. Apart from being inefficient and creating geometrical models that are much larger than they need to be, this can also be misleading since someone else viewing the model could conclude that all the design calculations and work to arrive at that specific component has already been done. If this is a building services component at an early design stage then this is almost certainly not the case.

The following slides address issues surrounding asset and information security and the processes, procedures and information required to critically evaluate the requirements for security in relation to an asset or a project. It is assumed that security threats must be considered as part of any BIM strategy and reviewed alongside the development of information processes for both asset management and project delivery. The scope encompasses security minded approaches to both digital environments and management of new and existing building assets.
Loss or disclosure of commercially sensitive information

It is important to understand the basics of security and why the concept of security forms a critical part of collaborative BIM. Security issues to be considered include those shown on screen.Implementing security approaches towards built assets will reduce the risk of the loss or disclosure of this sensitive information which could impact on safety and security; but also on the loss, theft or disclosure of commercially sensitive information or intellectual property.

The employer must appreciate that in respect of a built asset a holistic approach needs to address security around aspects such as people, process, technology and physical security. Overall security considerations group into 3 separate but linked domains. The digital domain (left hand side of diagram), the physical domain (right hand side of diagram), and the interface between them (central part of diagram).Digital:Integrity: Maintain the consistency, coherence and configuration of information and systems, and preventing unauthorized changes to themUtility: That asset information and systems remain usable and useful across the lifecycle of a built assetAuthenticity: Ensuring that inputs to and from built asset systems are genuine and have not been tampered withPhysical:Availability: To ensure that the asset information is accessible and reliableSafety: Prevention of harmful states which may lead to injury or loss of life in regard to the design, implementation, operation and maintenance of the built asset systemResilience: The ability of the asset information and systems to transform, renew and recover in a timely way in response to adverse eventsInterface:Confidentiality: The control of access and prevention of unauthorized access to information which may be sensitive in isolation or in aggregatePossession: Ensure that the prevention of unauthorized control, manipulation or interference is maintained
Apply triage for the appropriate security response
Triage is any procedure of assigning levels of priority to tasks or individuals to determine the most effective order in which to deal with them.

ISO 27001 sets out requirements for information security within an organization. ISO19650-5 focusses on the specific issues arising with built assets and with collaborative BIM. Understanding the security threat to an asset is important as it will allow a strategy to be put in place in order to manage it. It is recommended that the owner would set in place a process to ensure the security minded approach of the building has been considered. This starts with the security triage process. Two questions are considered: Is the owner’s built asset sensitive? And is there a neighbouring sensitive built asset? Yes or No answers to these two questions give four possible outcomes, identified here as St1 (the highest level of security-mindedness) to St4 (only standard levels of security-mindedness are required).Once this process has been worked through then the information management process can include the appropriate security measures.These might include:Hosting the CDE on the owner’s own servers, or at least within a particular jurisdictionRestricting the access to certain information containers because of their security sensitivityEnhanced levels of vetting for some or all of the individuals working on the project or asset management tasks

Some specific concerns: Surveys: The earliest stages of a project or asset management strategy should include security mindedness. Surveys may alert hostile parties to an imminent project and may include sensitive information from neighbouring facilities. Access to information: There should be a strategy toward management and monitoring of all information and systems within any file or database by any organization that has access to the AIM or CDE. The information should only be accessible to relevant personnel, contractors or those working on the asset and on a need-to-know basis. Storage and access to project asset information which is retained for the period required to comply with the legal or other regulatory requirements. Storing operation and maintenance information: There should be procedures for storing sensitive operation and maintenance information within the CDE or asset management data base (it is recommended that a risk assessment be undertaken). Storage and disposal of data: There should be arrangements for overseeing the secure storage and disposal of data and sensitive information at all times. These should be retained for asset management purposes.

The high-level strategy for managing security risk is the same for other areas of risk management. Within the framework of a risk management strategy, risks are assessed, then mitigated, then residual risks are controlled and the whole process is regularly reviewed. The cycle includes:Identify sensitive assets and informationAssess the value or impact of loss of the asset/informationAssess the threats and vulnerabilities to the asset/informationAssess the likelihood of the threatMitigationThen repeating the cycle.Mitigation of risks involves an initial review of whether the impacts are acceptable, and if not then mitigation measures to reduce the impact of loss, the vulnerabilities of the asset/information or the likelihood of the threat.The process of assessment and mitigation is iterative until an acceptable position has been reached within the constraints of time and cost available.

The security management plan will be informed by the security strategy and contain the following information: Policies which set out the strategy for the security strategy Procedures which compromise the detailed work and instructions for implementation and operation delivery of the processes of the security strategy Processes which are derived from security policies through the life of the assetAll these are developed in relation to risks and threats concerning people, processes, the physical asset and technology.Other topics to be covered in the security management plan are:Any impacts on the contracts arranged with suppliersThe security breach/incident management plan should also be devised hereIssues around storing and protecting the informationIssues around how information is to be shared (bearing in mind that this is a key requirement of the CDE)The security management plan is then used to inform the strategic brief or business case for the asset/project and also informs the various levels of information requirements.

Information management is an activity that needs people and organizations to carry out tasks, produce deliverables and oversee processes. The responsibility for each aspect of this has to be clearly defined as part of the preparation for managing information. Within a project or asset management team it is natural to group activities together into roles that specified individuals will be carrying out. But the intention behind information management according to ISO 19650 is that, after the initial learning and implementation has taken place, it is a more efficient process overall that requires less human resource than a non-standardized approach to managing information.ISO 19650 does not define any particular roles for personnel. Everything in the ISO is part of the information management function; some of this applies to the appointing party (client or asset owner) and some applies to the lead appointed parties.This section of the course considers the types of responsibility imposed by ISO 19650 and indicates how these might be organized by the different parties.

Roles and responsibilities have to be clearly defined so as to ensure the management of the BIM process is undertaken with ease. One method for clarifying the roles and responsibilities of possible parties includes the ‘RACI’ method. This abbreviation describes four types of engagement with a given task: R = Responsible: Takes the lead in carrying out the taskA = Accountable: Has overall ownership of the task, including sign-off of when the task has been completedC = Consulted: Has information and/or capability necessary or helpful to complete the taskI = Informed: Is notified of the results but does not need to be consulted while the task is being carried outThe RACI method is referenced within ISO 19650-2 in Annex A. This Annex is an assignment matrix of information management tasks. The rows in this matrix summarize the requirements of the normative clauses within ISO 19650-2, and the columns in this matrix show the four types of party referenced within ISO 19650-2.This matrix can be used by the whole project team, or by some of the organizations involved, to summarize the information management tasks that they are intending to be accountable for, responsible for, consulted on, and informed about.

There are a variety of functions and responsibilities that relate to the wider information management process, that can only be led by the appointing party. These include the core functions across the ISO 19650 series, including a smooth handover transition from delivery team to occupants and maintenance team (Soft Landings), and considerations of asset security.The role of the Soft Landings champion includes the ability to lead this smooth handover transition on behalf of all those involved (project and asset teams).The key to successful information management is clear and concise guidance from the appointing party in relation to the different levels of information requirements. ISO 19650-2 is very clear about the sequence of steps needed to arrive at coherent and consistent sets of delivery EIR for each appointed party. ISO 19650-3 is equally clear about the steps to establish operational EIR.Beyond establishing their requirements, the responsibilities of clients/owners then extend to making appropriate assessments of the capability of their prospective suppliers (appointed parties), by asking detailed questions and reviewing the answers given.Finally, because BIM places much more emphasis on getting design, construction and asset information generated in a systematic manner, the time to plan for this needs to be made available. Cases of ‘contract awarded on a Friday for a Monday start on design or on site’ do not allow any time for mobilization and planning and are not consistent with implementing information management according to ISO 19650.
Lead appointed party functions and responsibilities
Demonstrate abilityObey the CDE rulesDeliver and manage

The lead appointed parties involved in information management also have some specific functions that they need to carry out. They are in charge of pulling together their delivery team – of course this can include a mixture of external appointed parties and teams from within their own organization, and the delivery team can be any size. The lead appointed party is also leading on the response to the information requirements provided by the appointing party. Once the appointment is confirmed they oversee the information that is being produced within their team and be involved in delivering information exchanges to the appointing party.The responsibilities of appointed parties (designers, contractors, asset contractors, maintainers) on a BIM project or in BIM asset management fall into three areas.Firstly they have to demonstrate their understanding, experience and capacity to deliver information according to ISO 19650. They also have to start planning for how they are going to deliver the information the client/asset owner requires before they start work. Indeed they will have to start this planning as part of their tender, so they can demonstrate their understanding of the information requirements and the impacts they could have on the work.Secondly, they have to obey the ‘rules’ of the CDE process. On a project this means applying the information standard and information production methods and procedures that the appointing party (client) has specified. In the UK this would mean also applying the National Annex to ISO 19650-2 regarding container naming, status codes, revision codes and classification codes. They also need to work with other delivery team members to resolve problems around information (clashes in the models, discrepancies in documents or data) at their level of the supply chain hierarchy wherever possible and only escalate problems when they cannot solve them at task team level.Thirdly, the lead appointed parties who are leading multiple task teams in their delivery team have to deliver up and manage down. This largely applies to main contractors, but in these cases, they need to be able to provide BIM execution plans and information delivery plans and compile/issue task specific EIRs.

Information management in context to BIM is a critical component of the objectives of the Government in regard to delivering BIM on all centrally procured projects. Information management is also the lifeblood of any project requiring effective creation, management and collation of information across a BIM project. This section will review some of the common processes that are needed for information management to be successful. In some senses this is a reminder of some topics covered earlier during this course.

Collating and managing large amounts of information is a difficult task. To be able to effectively manage information, it is important to assess what type of information must be managed and how this information will be collated depending on the project and requirements. Information management can be defined as the collection and management of information from one or more sources and the distribution of that information effectively. Management means the organization of and control over the structure, processing and delivery of information. Information, as we know it today, can include both electronic and physical information. The organizational structure must be capable of managing this information throughout the information lifecycle regardless of source or format (data, paper documents, electronic documents, audio, social business, video, etc.) for delivery through multiple channels that may include digital or physical assets. Information management is a high-level responsibility that must be addressed by all members of an organization and effectively shared between those who require it at all times. Access to information is critical and the access to it must be managed and shared adequately. It is the responsibility of the owner to ensure that the information relating to the asset they own should be able to capture, manage, store, deliver, share, monitor use and update the information easily. This is critical to the success of an in-use asset or a project as it allows the information to be used as needed and when required.

Overview of the information management process (ISO 19650-1 Figure 11)This diagram summarizes the information management process captured in the concepts and principles of ISO 19650-1. These are implemented at project level through the requirements listed in ISO 19650-2 and at asset/facility management level through the requirements in ISO 19650-3.There are several elements to this diagram:The inner green wedge shape: This indicates that information quantity grows during a project (as it progresses through its plan of work stages) and during asset management (as trigger events prompt collection of new and updated information). It also indicates that a project should always start with some information, and that when the project is complete not all the project information model will be relevant or needed for asset management.The outer blue pathway: This indicates that the process of making any of the appointments goes through a structured process of defining requirements, reviewing the capability and capacity of those being considered for the work, mobilization of each delivery team, and then production of the information itself.The delivery of information to the appointing party: This comes from delivery team decisions (the green diamonds) and is exchanged with the appointing party (green circles) with before and after reviews for completeness and quality (purple arrows). The information is used by the appointing party or its stakeholders to make their key decisions regarding the project or the asset.Please see your delegate materials for a larger version of the diagram.

Each information exchange during the project/asset lifecycle is a collection of relevant task team information coordinated in each delivery team. Some information exchange is between the delivery team and the appointing party, shown as the dark green circles in the slide. Other information exchange is within the delivery team – the light green circles – and these are used by the lead appointed party to manage the project or asset management activities.The appointed parties within each delivery team might be formed into a multi-layer hierarchy, as indicated. Or the delivery team might consist of just one task team, in which case that organization will also be the lead appointed party.At each information exchange there is a before and after review process, to make sure that information being received by the appointing party aligns with the information requirements and then to make sure that the information to start the next stage or task is sufficient for the work to start. These reviews are indicated by the green circular arrows either side of the information exchange.For larger version of diagram, please refer to your course material: Information exchange to support appointing party key decisions.

Information according to ISO 19650 is a whole lifecycle activity. It covers both the delivery phase of an asset (a project or similar major piece of work) and the operational phase of an asset (including day-to-day operation and maintenance). This diagram illustrates how these two elements of the whole lifecycle connect with each other at points A and C. These points represent the transfer of information between asset and PIMs and processes.This diagram also shows how information management exists within and has to connect to some wider management processes. These include project and asset management, for example through the implementation of ISO 21500 and ISO 55000, and also organizational management such as ISO 9001. There are, of course, many other management systems that might be relevant to particular organizations, assets and projects, but these are not shown on the diagram. These wider management systems are important, since they give the opportunity to connect information management to other parts of the organization’s business to support the beneficial outcomes that information management can deliver.

It is important to understand that at all key stages across the asset lifecycle there are information delivery processes that will need to be defined, created and managed. The slide summarizes the main information management materials that are used at the principal lifecycle stages. Many of these items are specific to project or asset appointments, so will arise whenever an appointment is being made and will be managed during that appointment. Information management is a process that comes under change control. In other words there has to be a formal process for dealing with changes to the guiding documents such as the information requirements, the BIM execution plans and the information delivery plans (MIDP/TIDP).

Implementing information management according to ISO 19650 at a practical level is a substantial task, but one that promises to deliver large improvements in supply chain efficiency, open up new markets and secure leading-edge clients who are appreciating the value that the digital twin of their physical asset can provide for them.This final part of the course is about consolidating the topics we have discussed over the course, and reflecting on how the appropriate organizational change could be achieved.

Organizational change management can be applied to any organization in relation to BIM in general, and information management according to ISO 19650 in particular. It is important to think about the change on three levels, the why, the what and the how. In the slide, the why is represented by the input and output arrows. These represent push from outside the organization (the government mandate, changes taking place elsewhere in the sector, and external opportunities such as for exports), and pull from within the organization (the desire for growth, for increased sustainability or for increased profitability). Each organization will need to understand for itself which of these external and internal pressures are most important and most urgent. The what and the how are represented by the central BIM web. This highlights the key areas of the organization’s business that will drive the BIM response and may need to change or be developed to support BIM implementation.It is recommended that planning to implement BIM starts with a plan for your organization. This should involve all the factors discussed on this slide (or whatever different or additional factors are relevant to you). In fact, deciding what these factors are is the first step in your organization’s own internal BIM implementation plan.
Work out what resources you need
Set out a plan for acquiring the additional resources you need
Monitor the implementation of the plan

Planning at a corporate level is critical for successful information management:Define your information management objectives: How will information management improve what you do (in terms of managing assets, managing projects, delivering projects)?Work out what resources you need (people, written procedures, training/skills, IT software/hardware) and how these compare with what you already haveSet out a plan for acquiring the additional resources you need. Where expenditure or change to work processes is needed, what do you need to do to get approval for these?Monitor the implementation of the plan. Check that milestones and objectives are being achievedUpdate the plan as needed. Maybe progress is not as fast as was expected, or maybe clients and stakeholders are pushing for faster implementationPlanning is also needed from individual teams and disciplines for their information management on a specific project or asset management activity. These plans need to focus on the project/asset specifics in the EIR and the details contained in the BEP.BIM provides a good opportunity for any organization to review its business processes. This applies to project clients, asset owners, and design, construction and asset management supply chains.

Planning is also needed from individual teams and disciplines for their information management on a specific project or asset management activity. These plans need to focus on the project/asset specifics in the EIR and the details contained in the BEP.BIM provides a good opportunity for any organization to review its business processes. This applies to project clients, asset owners, and design, construction and asset management supply chains.
Sustainability and corporate social responsibility processes

As BIM cannot really be considered in complete isolation, as it affects and is affected by other organizational processes, then a process review that starts with information processes should spread outward to other areas of the organization. These might include:Recruitment and training processesBoard-level organizational review processesSustainability and corporate social responsibility processesQuality managementHealth and safety management
A holistic approach to change management

This slide shows how successful change management requires many different facets to be brought together. If any one of them is missing, then the result is a failure to implement change for varying reasons.If staff see no vision, then they are confused as to why the change is needed. If the skills are not present then there is anxiety as to whether the outcome will be successful. If there are no incentives (not necessarily financial – this includes reassurance that jobs will be secure or new posts created) then resistance arises. If the resources to implement change are not provided, then there is frustration. And if there is no plan then there will be false starts probably leading to the change being abandoned.Feedback gained during the change process needs to be incorporated into revised plans for change, and where formal change management has been used before then the lessons from these experiences can also be beneficial.Diagram from Knoster, T., Villa, R., & Thousand, J.
